result:
I've been thinking about the strange beauty of fog lately. There's something transformative about how it rolls in, erasing the sharp edges of the world and replacing them with soft uncertainty. Buildings become suggestions, trees turn into sketches, and people emerge and vanish like thoughts you can't quite grasp.

Fog makes me wonder about clarity—how much we actually need it. We spend so much time trying to see everything clearly, to understand completely, to map out every detail. But fog forces a different kind of attention. You have to focus on what's immediately present, trust your memory of what lies beyond, and accept that some things will remain hidden.

I find myself drawn to foggy mornings the way others are drawn to sunny days. There's a peculiar comfort in that gray embrace, a permission to exist in ambiguity. The world becomes smaller and larger simultaneously—smaller because you can only see so far, larger because anything could exist in that soft blindness.

Perhaps fog appeals to me because it mirrors how knowledge itself works. We move through understanding with limited visibility, catching glimpses of truth before it dissolves back into uncertainty. We navigate by instinct and accumulated wisdom, hoping we're heading in the right direction.

When the fog finally lifts and the world snaps back into harsh focus, I sometimes feel a twinge of loss. That gentle mystery recedes, leaving behind the demanding clarity of another ordinary day. But I know it will return, bringing its questions and quietude.
